The **X chromosome** is one of the two [sex chromosomes](/wiki/Sex_chromosome "Sex chromosome") in many organisms, including mammals, and is found in both males and females. It is a part of the [XY sex-determination system](/wiki/XY_sex-determination_system "XY sex-determination system") and [XO sex-determination system](/wiki/XO_sex-determination_system "XO sex-determination system"). The X chromosome was named for its unique properties by early researchers, which resulted in the naming of its counterpart [Y chromosome](/wiki/Y_chromosome "Y chromosome"), for the next letter in the alphabet, following its subsequent discovery.[[3]](#cite_note-nyt-angier-3)

## Discovery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X_chromosome&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Discovery")]

It was first noted that the X chromosome was special in 1890 by [Hermann Henking](/wiki/Hermann_Henking "Hermann Henking") in Leipzig. Henking was studying the testicles of  [*Pyrrhocoris*](/wiki/Pyrrhocoris_apterus "Pyrrhocoris apterus") and noticed that one chromosome did not take part in [meiosis](/wiki/Meiosis "Meiosis"). Chromosomes are so named because of their ability to take up [staining](/wiki/Staining "Staining") (*chroma* in Greek means *color*). Although the X chromosome could be stained just as well as the others, Henking was unsure whether it was a different class of the object and consequently named it *X element*,[[4]](#cite_note-Schwartz-4) which later became X chromosome after it was established that it was indeed a chromosome.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The idea that the X chromosome was named after its similarity to the letter "X" is mistaken. All chromosomes normally appear as an amorphous blob under the microscope and take on a well-defined shape only during mitosis. This shape is vaguely X-shaped for all chromosomes. It is entirely coincidental that the [Y chromosome](/wiki/Y_chromosome "Y chromosome"), during [mitosis](/wiki/Mitosis "Mitosis"), has two very short branches which can look merged under the microscope and appear as the descender of a Y-shape.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

It was first suggested that the X chromosome was involved in sex determination by [Clarence Erwin McClung](/wiki/Clarence_Erwin_McClung "Clarence Erwin McClung") in 1901. After comparing his work on [locusts](/wiki/Locust "Locust") with Henking's and others, McClung noted that only half the sperm received an X chromosome. He called this chromosome an *accessory chromosome*, and insisted (correctly) that it was a *proper* chromosome, and theorized (incorrectly) that it was the male-determining chromosome.[[4]](#cite_note-Schwartz-4)

## Humans

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X_chromosome&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Humans")]

### Function

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X_chromosome&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Function")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Sd4hi-unten-crop.jpg/250px-Sd4hi-unten-crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sd4hi-unten-crop.jpg)

Nucleus of a female amniotic fluid cell. Top: Both X-chromosome territories are detected by [FISH](/wiki/Fluorescence_in_situ_hybridization "Fluorescence in situ hybridization"). Shown is a single optical section made with a [confocal microscope](/wiki/Confocal_laser_scanning_microscopy "Confocal laser scanning microscopy"). Bottom: Same nucleus stained with [DAPI](/wiki/DAPI "DAPI") and recorded with a [CCD camera](/wiki/Charge-coupled_device "Charge-coupled device"). The Barr body is indicated by the arrow, it identifies the inactive X (Xi).

The X chromosome in humans spans more than 153 million [base pairs](/wiki/Base_pairs "Base pairs") (the building material of [DNA](/wiki/DNA "DNA")). It represents about 800 protein-coding genes compared to the Y chromosome containing about 107 protein-coding genes (42 exclusive protein-coding genes),[[7]](#cite_note-7) out of 20,000–25,000 total genes in the human genome.
Each person usually has one pair of sex chromosomes in each cell. Females typically have two X chromosomes, whereas males typically have one X and one [Y chromosome](/wiki/Y_chromosome "Y chromosome"). Both males and females retain one of their mother's X chromosomes, and females retain their second X chromosome from their father. Since the father retains his X chromosome from his mother, a human female has one X chromosome from her paternal grandmother (father's side), and one X chromosome from her mother. This inheritance pattern [follows the Fibonacci numbers](#Inheritance_pattern) at a given ancestral depth[[8]](#cite_note-xcs-8)[[a]](#cite_note-9)

[Genetic disorders](/wiki/Genetic_disorder "Genetic disorder") that are due to [mutations](/wiki/Mutation "Mutation") in genes on the X chromosome are described as [X linked](/wiki/X-linked_recessive_inheritance "X-linked recessive inheritance"). If the X chromosome has a genetic disease gene, it always causes illness in male patients, since males have only one X chromosome and therefore only one copy of each gene. Females, instead, require both X chromosomes to have the illness, and as a result could potentially only be a carrier of genetic illness, since their second X chromosome overrides the first. For example, [hemophilia A](/wiki/Hemophilia_A "Hemophilia A") and [B](/wiki/Hemophilia_B "Hemophilia B") and [congenital red–green color blindness](/wiki/Congenital_red%E2%80%93green_color_blindness "Congenital red–green color blindness") run in families this way.

The X chromosome carries hundreds of genes but few, if any, of these have anything to do directly with sex determination. Early in [embryonic](/wiki/Embryo "Embryo") development in females, one of the two X chromosomes is permanently inactivated in nearly all somatic cells (cells other than [egg](/wiki/Ovum "Ovum") and [sperm](/wiki/Spermatozoon "Spermatozoon") cells). This phenomenon is called [X-inactivation](/wiki/X-inactivation "X-inactivation") or [Lyonization](/wiki/Lyonization "Lyonization"), and creates a [Barr body](/wiki/Barr_body "Barr body"). If X-inactivation in the somatic cell meant a complete de-functionalizing of one of the X-chromosomes, it would ensure that females, like males, had only one functional copy of the X chromosome in each somatic cell. This was previously assumed to be the case. However, recent research suggests that the [Barr body](/wiki/Barr_body "Barr body") may be more biologically active than was previously supposed.[[9]](#cite_note-Carrel-10)

The partial [inactivation of the X-chromosome](/wiki/X-inactivation "X-inactivation") is due to repressive [heterochromatin](/wiki/Heterochromatin "Heterochromatin") that compacts the DNA and prevents the expression of most genes. Heterochromatin compaction is regulated by Polycomb Repressive Complex 2 ([PRC2](/wiki/PRC2 "PRC2")).[[10]](#cite_note-pmid28758948-11)

#### Number of genes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X_chromosome&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Number of genes")]

The following are some of the gene count estimates of human X chromosome. Because researchers use different approaches to [genome annotation](/wiki/Genome_annotation "Genome annotation") their predictions of the [number of genes](/wiki/Number_of_genes "Number of genes") on each chromosome varies (for technical details, see [gene prediction](/wiki/Gene_prediction "Gene prediction")). Among various projects, the collaborative consensus coding sequence project ([CCDS](/wiki/Consensus_CDS_Project "Consensus CDS Project")) takes an extremely conservative strategy. So CCDS's gene number prediction represents a lower bound on the total number of human protein-coding genes.[[11]](#cite_note-pmid20441615-12)

### Structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X_chromosome&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Structure")]

It is theorized by Ross et al. 2005 and Ohno 1967 that the X chromosome is at least partially derived from the autosomal (non-sex-related) genome of other mammals, evidenced from interspecies genomic sequence alignments.

The X chromosome is notably larger and has a more active [euchromatin](/wiki/Euchromatin "Euchromatin") region than its [Y chromosome](/wiki/Y_chromosome "Y chromosome") counterpart. Further comparison of the X and Y reveal regions of homology between the two. However, the corresponding region in the Y appears far shorter and lacks regions that are conserved in the X throughout primate species, implying a genetic degeneration for Y in that region. Because males have only one X chromosome, they are more likely to have an X chromosome-related disease.

It is estimated that about 10% of the genes encoded by the X chromosome are associated with a family of "CT" genes, so named because they encode for markers found in both tumor cells (in cancer patients) as well as in the human [testis](/wiki/Testis "Testis") (in healthy patients).[[19]](#cite_note-ross-20)

### Role in disease

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X_chromosome&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Role in disease")]

#### Numerical abnormalities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X_chromosome&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Numerical abnormalities")]

[Klinefelter syndrome](/wiki/Klinefelter_syndrome "Klinefelter syndrome"):

* Klinefelter syndrome is caused by the presence of one or more extra copies of the X chromosome in a male's cells.
* Males with Klinefelter syndrome typically have one extra copy of the X chromosome in each cell, for a total of two X chromosomes and one Y chromosome (47,XXY). It is less common for affected males to have two or three extra X chromosomes (48,XXXY or 49,XXXXY) or extra copies of both the X and Y chromosomes (48,XXYY) in each cell. The extra genetic material may lead to tall stature, learning and reading disabilities, and other medical problems. Each extra X chromosome lowers the child's [IQ](/wiki/IQ "IQ") by about 15 points,[[20]](#cite_note-21)[[21]](#cite_note-22) which means that the average IQ in Klinefelter syndrome is in general in the normal range, although below average. When additional X and/or Y chromosomes are present in 48,XXXY, 48,XXYY, or 49,XXXXY, developmental delays and cognitive difficulties can be more severe and [mild intellectual disability](/wiki/Mild_intellectual_disability "Mild intellectual disability") may be present.
* Klinefelter syndrome can also result from an extra X chromosome in only some of the body's cells. These cases are called mosaic 46,XY/47,XXY.

[Trisomy X](/wiki/Trisomy_X "Trisomy X")

* This syndrome results from an extra copy of the X chromosome in each of a female's cells. Females with trisomy X have three X chromosomes, for a total of 47 chromosomes per cell. The average [IQ](/wiki/IQ "IQ") of females with this syndrome is 90, while the average [IQ](/wiki/IQ "IQ") of unaffected siblings is 100.[[22]](#cite_note-23) Their stature on average is taller than normal females. They are fertile and their children do not inherit the condition.[[23]](#cite_note-24)
* Females with more than one extra copy of the X chromosome (48, [tetrasomy X](/wiki/Tetrasomy_X "Tetrasomy X") or 49, [pentasomy X](/wiki/Pentasomy_X "Pentasomy X")) have been identified, but these conditions are rare.

[Turner syndrome](/wiki/Turner_syndrome "Turner syndrome"):

* This results when each of a female's cells has one normal X chromosome and the other sex chromosome is missing or altered. The missing genetic material affects development and causes the features of the condition, including short stature and infertility.
* About half of individuals with Turner syndrome have [monosomy](/wiki/Monosomy "Monosomy") X (45,X), which means each cell in a woman's body has only one copy of the X chromosome instead of the usual two copies. Turner syndrome can also occur if one of the sex chromosomes is partially missing or rearranged rather than completely missing. Some women with Turner syndrome have a chromosomal change in only some of their cells. These cases are called Turner syndrome mosaics (45,X/46,XX).

#### X-linked recessive disorders

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X_chromosome&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: X-linked recessive disorders")]

[Sex linkage](/wiki/Sex_linkage "Sex linkage") was first discovered in insects, e.g., [T. H. Morgan](/wiki/Thomas_Hunt_Morgan "Thomas Hunt Morgan")'s 1910 discovery of the pattern of inheritance of the white eyes mutation in *[Drosophila melanogaster](/wiki/Drosophila_melanogaster "Drosophila melanogaster")*.[[24]](#cite_note-25) Such discoveries helped to explain x-linked disorders in humans, e.g., [haemophilia](/wiki/Haemophilia "Haemophilia") A and B, [adrenoleukodystrophy](/wiki/Adrenoleukodystrophy "Adrenoleukodystrophy"), and [red-green color blindness](/wiki/Color_blindness "Color blindness").

#### Other disorders

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X_chromosome&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Other disorders")]

Further information: [X-linked recessive](/wiki/X-linked_recessive "X-linked recessive") and [X-linked dominant](/wiki/X-linked_dominant "X-linked dominant")

[XX male syndrome](/wiki/XX_male_syndrome "XX male syndrome") is a rare disorder, where the [SRY](/wiki/SRY "SRY") region of the Y chromosome has recombined to be located on one of the X chromosomes. As a result, the XX combination after fertilization has the same effect as a XY combination, resulting in a male. However, the other genes of the X chromosome cause feminization as well.

[X-linked endothelial corneal dystrophy](/wiki/X-linked_endothelial_corneal_dystrophy "X-linked endothelial corneal dystrophy") is an extremely rare disease of cornea associated with Xq25 region. [Lisch epithelial corneal dystrophy](/wiki/Lisch_epithelial_corneal_dystrophy "Lisch epithelial corneal dystrophy") is associated with Xp22.3.

[Megalocornea 1](/wiki/Megalocornea "Megalocornea") is associated with Xq21.3-q22[*[medical citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Identifying_reliable_sources_(medicine) "Wikipedia:Identifying reliable sources (medicine)")*]

[Adrenoleukodystrophy](/wiki/Adrenoleukodystrophy "Adrenoleukodystrophy"), a rare and fatal disorder that is carried by the mother on the x-cell. Its most severe cerebral form, CCALD, typically affects boys between the ages of 4 and 12 and destroys the protective cell surrounding the nerves, [myelin](/wiki/Myelin "Myelin"), in the brain and usually, within two years after diagnosis, most boys with adrenoleukodystrophy die or enter a vegetative state. The disease also commonly causes adrenal insufficiency which manifests as Addison's disease and another common phenotype is adrenomyeloneuropathy AMN, which usually presents in older men between the ages of 20–40. The female carrier hardly shows any symptoms because females have a copy of the x-cell. This disorder causes a once healthy boy to lose all abilities to walk, talk, see, hear, and even swallow.

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=X_chromosome&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-9)** [Luke Hutchison](/w/index.php?title=Luke_Hutchison&action=edit&redlink=1 "Luke Hutchison (page does not exist)") noticed that a number of possible ancestors on the X chromosome inheritance line at a given ancestral generation follows the [Fibonacci sequence](/wiki/Fibonacci_sequence "Fibonacci sequence").[[8]](#cite_note-xcs-8) A male individual has an X chromosome, which he received from his mother, and a [Y chromosome](/wiki/Y_chromosome "Y chromosome"), which he received from his father. The male counts as the "origin" of his own X chromosome (![{\displaystyle F_{1}=1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c374ba08c140de90c6cbb4c9b9fcd26e3f99ef56)), and at his parents' generation, his X chromosome came from a single parent (![{\displaystyle F_{2}=1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/fdf100a6879452aff05a6027ad4f36029f360dcc)). The male's mother received one X chromosome from her mother (the son's maternal grandmother), and one from her father (the son's maternal grandfather), so two grandparents contributed to the male descendant's X chromosome (![{\displaystyle F_{3}=2}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/1caf4ec3fa730d75d5508487f92ebe0a64307a4a)). The maternal grandfather received his X chromosome from his mother, and the maternal grandmother received X chromosomes from both of her parents, so three great-grandparents contributed to the male descendant's X chromosome (![{\displaystyle F_{4}=3}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/3fcb7ddc635c556db90a8c277ce6b45e6d4aa185)). Five great-great-grandparents contributed to the male descendant's X chromosome (![{\displaystyle F_{5}=5}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/767c16dde6476b8fd6618435d70266a3747e426f)), etc. (Note that this assumes that all ancestors of a given descendant are independent, but if any genealogy is traced far enough back in time, ancestors begin to appear on multiple lines of the genealogy, until eventually, a [population founder](/wiki/Founder_effect "Founder effect") appears on all lines of the genealogy.).
